en.extract:
The Gouldian finch (Chloebia gouldiae), also known as the Gould's finch or the rainbow finch, is a colourful passerine bird that is native to Australia.


Taxonomy
The Gouldian finch was described by British ornithologist John Gould in 1844 as Amadina gouldiae, in honour of his deceased wife Elizabeth. Specimens of the bird were sent to him by British naturalist Benjamin Bynoe, although they had been described some years before by French naturalists Jacques Bernard Hombron and Honoré Jacquinot. It is also known as the rainbow finch, Gould's finch, or sometimes just Gould. The Gouldian finch is sister to the parrotfinches in the genus Erythrura.


Description
Both sexes are brightly coloured with black, green, yellow, and red markings. The females tend to be less brightly coloured. One major difference between the sexes is that the male's chest is purple, while the female's is a lighter mauve.
Gouldian finches are about 125–140 mm (4.9–5.5 in) long. Their heads may be red, black, or yellow. Formerly considered three different kinds of finches, it is now known that these are colour variants of one species that exist in the wild. Selective breeding has also developed mutations (blue, yellow and silver instead of a green back) in both body and breast colour.
There are several "prominent rounded tubercles" with an "opalescent lustre" at the back of the gape of nestlings. These tubercles are commonly (and incorrectly) described as phosphorescent in spite of much scientific evidence to the contrary. It is believed that these tubercles simply reflect light and are not luminescent.


Distribution and habitat

Gouldian finches are native to northern Australia, in particular the Kimberley and Northern Territory.
Prior to the Australian government's ban on the export of Australian fauna, Gouldian finches were exported worldwide, which has resulted in viable captive breeding populations being held in many countries.


Conservation status
This species has been considered an Endangered species by the Australian Government in the last two "Endangered Species Act"s - the Australian Endangered Species Protection Act (ESPA) of 1992 and the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation (EPBC) Act, 1999. Its status on the IUCN Red List is currently Least concern, but it was considered to be endangered previously: Threatened in 1988, EN in six assessments between 1994 and 2008, and Near threatened in three assessments between 2012 and 2016.
The number of Gouldian finches in the wild decreased dramatically in the 20th century due to human-caused habitat loss. The population went from hundreds of thousands in the early 20th century to 2,500 or fewer by the 1980s. The current estimated population continues to be 2,500 or fewer birds. Early research suggested that a parasite called the air sac mite was responsible for the species' decline, but the mite is no longer considered a major factor.  The primary threat to wild Gouldian finch populations is an increase in extensive wildfires in the late dry season of its native habitat, which negatively impacts the availability of both tree hollows for breeding, and the seeds that comprise the bulk of the Gouldian finch's diet. Cyclones and climate change have also negatively impacted tree hollow availability in the Northern Territory.


Behaviour
Outside the breeding season, Gouldian finches often join mixed flocks consisting of long-tailed finches and masked finches. Flocks can consist of up to 1,000–2,000 individuals. During the breeding season, they are normally found on rough scree slopes where vegetation is sparse. In the dry season, they are much more nomadic and will move to wherever their food and water can be found.


Feeding
Like other finches, the Gouldian finch is a seed eater. They eat up to 30% of their bodyweight each day. During the breeding season, Gouldian finches mainly feed on ripe and half-ripe grass seeds of sorghum. During the dry season, they mainly forage on the ground for seeds. During the wet season, spinifex grass seed (Triodia sp.) is an important part of their diet. So far Gouldians have been recorded eating six different species of grass seed, but researchers have yet to find evidence of insect consumption.


Breeding

Gouldian finches will usually make their nests in tree hollows. They usually breed in the early part of the dry season, when there is plenty of food available. When a male is courting a female, he bobs about and ruffles his feathers in an attempt to show off his bright colours. He will expand his chest and fluff out the feathers on his forehead. After mating, the female will lay a clutch of about 4–8 eggs. Both parents help brood the eggs during the daytime, and it is the female who stays on the eggs at night. When the eggs hatch, both parents care for the young. Gouldian finches leave the nest after between 19 and 25 days and are completely independent at 40 days old.
Gouldian finches have brightly coloured gapes and call loudly when the parent birds return so that they are able to find and feed their mouths in the dark nest.
It has been shown that female Gouldian finches from Northern Australia can control the sex of their offspring by choosing mates according to their head colour. A certain amount of genetic incompatibility between black and red-headed birds can result in high mortality (up to 80%) in female offspring when birds of different head colours mate. If the female mates with a finch of different head colour, this genetic incompatibility can be addressed by over-producing sons, up to a ratio of four males to one female. This is one of the first proven instances of birds biasing the sex of their offspring to overcome genetic weaknesses.


Aviculture
Gouldian finches are a popular species in aviculture because of their striking colours and low care requirements. Gouldian finches get along well with other species of grass finch and some other docile species of bird, such as waxbills and parrot finches.


Trapping for aviculture
In the Kimberley District of Western Australia, where most wild Gouldian finch were trapped for aviculture, it was often reported as one of the more common of the eleven finch species.  Until 1977, it was trapped in greater numbers than any other finch.  From 1897, when finch trapping started in the Kimberley, it was the most sought after finch by trappers and the most desired by fanciers.  Between 1934 and 1939, the Gouldian finch was the most exported single finch species.  The Perth Zoo exported 22,064 finches of which 12,509 were Gouldian.  Private dealers exported 35,315 finches, of which 14,504 were Gouldian.  The number of finches taken in the 1958 finch trapping season was the largest for one year, of the 38,649 finches taken, 11,286 were Gouldian.  The last licensed trapping of Gouldian finch in Western Australia was on 15 November 1981.  In that year's finch trapping season, of the 23,450 finches taken 1,054 were Gouldian. However, it is now illegal to export these birds from Australia.


In popular culture
The Gouldian finch is used as the basis of the ViewSonic logo.

Gouldian finch mutations


References

The '''Gouldian finch''' ('''''Chloebia gouldiae'''''), also known as the '''Gould's finch''' or the '''rainbow finch''', is a colourful [[passerine]] bird that is native to [[Australia]].

==Taxonomy==
The Gouldian finch was described by British ornithologist [[John Gould]] in 1844 as ''Amadina gouldiae'',<ref name="AFD">{{cite web|url=https://biodiversity.org.au/afd/taxa/Erythrura_%28Chloebia%29_gouldiae |title=Species ''Erythrura (Chloebia) gouldiae'' (Gould, 1844) |author=Australian Biological Resources Study|date=6 May 2016|work=Australian Faunal Directory|publisher=Department of the Environment, Water, Heritage and the Arts, Australian Government|access-date=28 August 2018|location=Canberra, Australian Capital Territory}}</ref> in honour of his deceased wife [[Elizabeth Gould (illustrator)|Elizabeth]].<ref>{{cite journal | url=http://aviculturalsocietynsw.org/_inMemoriam/GouldJohn1804-1881.htm | title=The Goulds, John Gould (1804–1881) | journal=Avicultural Review | publisher=The Avicultural Society of New South Wales (ASNSW) | date=January 1998 | volume=15 | issue=1 | archive-date=12 April 2018 | access-date=25 August 2018 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180412082836/http://aviculturalsocietynsw.org/_inMemoriam/GouldJohn1804-1881.htm | url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetaviary.com/part-1-an-introduction-to-the-gouldian-finch/|title=Part 1: An introduction to the Gouldian finch – Planet Aviary|website=planetaviary.com|date=17 February 2016 |language=en-GB|access-date=2018-08-25}}</ref> Specimens of the bird were sent to him by British naturalist [[Benjamin Bynoe]], although they had been described some years before by French naturalists [[Jacques Bernard Hombron]] and [[Honoré Jacquinot]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.gouldianfinches.eu/en/ethology/discovering-and-characterisation-of-species/|title=Gouldian Finch discovery|website=GouldianFinches.eu}}</ref> It is also known as the rainbow finch, Gould's finch, or sometimes just Gould.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.efinch.com/species/gould.htm|title=The Gouldian Finch – Chloebia gouldiae|website=www.efinch.com|access-date=2018-08-27}}</ref> The Gouldian finch is [[sister taxon|sister]] to the parrotfinches in the genus ''[[Erythrura]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Olsson | first1=Urban | last2=Alström | first2=Per | author2-link=Alström | date=2020 | title=A comprehensive phylogeny and taxonomic evaluation of the waxbills (Aves: Estrildidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=146 | article-number=106757 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2020.106757| pmid=32028027 | s2cid=211048731 | doi-access=free | bibcode=2020MolPE.14606757O }}</ref>

==Description==
Both sexes are brightly coloured with black, green, yellow, and red markings. The females tend to be less brightly coloured. One major difference between the sexes is that the male's chest is [[purple]], while the female's is a lighter [[mauve]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.arkive.org/gouldian-finch/erythrura-gouldiae/|title=Gouldian finch - ''Erythrura gouldiae'' - ARKive|date=29 January 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090129091121/http://www.arkive.org/gouldian-finch/erythrura-gouldiae/ |archive-date=29 January 2009 }}</ref>

Gouldian finches are about {{cvt|125|–|140|mm}} long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.diamante-de-gould.com/en/caracteristicas/identificacion.html|title=Gouldian Finch Identification – Gouldian Finch|website=www.diamante-de-gould.com|language=en|access-date=2018-08-26|archive-date=7 December 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191207095708/http://www.diamante-de-gould.com/en/caracteristicas/identificacion.html}}</ref> Their heads may be red, black, or yellow. Formerly considered three different kinds of finches, it is now known that these are [[polymorphism (biology)|colour variants]] of one species that exist in the wild.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Southern H.N. |year=1945|title= Polymorphism in ''Poephila gouldiae''|journal= J. Genet.|volume= 47|pages=51–157|url=http://www.ias.ac.in/jarch/jgenet/47/51.pdf|doi=10.1007/BF02989037|s2cid=38518147}}</ref> [[Selective breeding]] has also developed mutations (blue, yellow and silver instead of a green back) in both body and breast colour.<ref>{{Cite web | url=https://finchstuff.com/gouldianmutations |title = Gouldian Finch colour mutations}}</ref>

There are several "prominent rounded tubercles" with an "opalescent lustre" at the back of the gape of nestlings. These tubercles are commonly (and incorrectly) described as [[Phosphorescence|phosphorescent]] in spite of much scientific evidence to the contrary.<ref name="McAtee W.L. 1947 207–213">{{cite journal|author=McAtee W.L. |date=July 1947 |title=Luminosity in Birds |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_american-midland-naturalist_1947-07_38_1/page/206 |journal=American Midland Naturalist|volume=38 |number=1 |pages=207–213|jstor=2421636|doi=10.2307/2421636}}</ref> It is believed that these tubercles simply reflect light and are not [[Luminescence|luminescent]].<ref name="McAtee W.L. 1947 207–213"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Erythrura gouldiae -Frankfurt Zoo -black headed-8a-3cp.jpg|thumb|right|Black-headed male Gouldian finch]]
Gouldian finches are native to northern Australia,<ref>{{Cite news |last=Cox |first=Lisa |date=2022-09-04 |title=Tanya Plibersek urged to save Gouldian finches from NT defence development |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2022/sep/05/tanya-plibersek-urged-to-save-gouldian-finches-from-nt-defence-development |access-date=2023-08-13 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> in particular [[Kimberley (Western Australia)|the Kimberley]] and [[Northern Territory]].<ref name=":0" />

Prior to the Australian government's ban on the export of Australian fauna,<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://environment.gov.au/biodiversity/wildlife-trade/natives/exporting-live-australian-natives|title=Department of the Environment and Energy|website=Department of the Environment and Energy|language=en|access-date=2018-08-25}}</ref> Gouldian finches were exported worldwide, which has resulted in viable captive breeding populations being held in many countries.{{Citation needed|date=October 2023}}

===Conservation status===
This species has been considered an [[Endangered species]] by the Australian Government in the last two "Endangered Species Act"s - the Australian Endangered Species Protection Act (ESPA) of 1992<ref name=ESPA>{{cite web|url=https://www.legislation.gov.au/C2004A04485/latest/text |title=Endangered Species Protection Act 1992|date=5 December 1999 |publisher=Australian Government}}</ref> and the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation (EPBC) Act, 1999.<ref name=EPBC/> Its status on the [[IUCN Red List]] is currently [[Least concern]], but it was considered to be endangered previously: Threatened in 1988, EN in six assessments between 1994 and 2008, and [[Near threatened]] in three assessments between 2012 and 2016.<ref name=IUCN/>

The number of Gouldian finches in the wild decreased dramatically in the 20th century due to human-caused habitat loss.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Tidemann |first=Sonia C. |date=March 1996 |title=Causes of the decline of the Gouldian Finch ''Erythrura gouldiae'' |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=6 |issue=1 |pages=49–61 |doi=10.1017/S0959270900001301 |issn=|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=":0" /> The population went from hundreds of thousands in the early 20th century to 2,500 or fewer by the 1980s.<ref name=":3" /> The current estimated population continues to be 2,500 or fewer birds.<ref name=":0" /> Early research suggested that a parasite called the air sac mite was responsible for the species' decline, but the mite is no longer considered a major factor.{{Citation needed|date=October 2023}}  The primary threat to wild Gouldian finch populations is an increase in extensive wildfires in the late dry season of its native habitat, which negatively impacts the availability of both tree hollows for breeding, and the seeds that comprise the bulk of the Gouldian finch's diet.<ref name=":0">{{Cite news|url=http://www.wwf.org.au/what-we-do/species/gouldian-finch#gs.SRXFq2I|title=Gouldian finch – WWF-Australia|access-date=2018-08-25}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.australianwildlife.org/wildlife/gouldian-finch.aspx|title=Australian Wildlife Conservancy|website=www.australianwildlife.org|language=en|access-date=2018-08-25}}</ref> Cyclones and climate change have also negatively impacted tree hollow availability in the [[Northern Territory]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Abbatangelo |first=Ben |date=2023-07-11 |title=Binybara is not just home to the Gouldian finch. It's part of the Larrakia nation. And it deserves protection |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2023/jul/11/binybara-is-not-just-home-to-the-gouldian-finch-its-part-of-the-larrakia-nation-and-it-deserves-protection |access-date=2023-08-13 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
Outside the breeding season, Gouldian finches often join mixed flocks consisting of [[long-tailed finch]]es and [[masked finch]]es. Flocks can consist of up to 1,000–2,000 individuals.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/gouldianfinch.html#distribution|title=Gouldian Finches or Rainbow Finches {{!}} Beauty of Birds|website=www.beautyofbirds.com|language=en|access-date=2018-08-30}}</ref> During the breeding season, they are normally found on rough scree slopes where vegetation is sparse. In the dry season, they are much more nomadic and will move to wherever their food and water can be found.<ref name=":2" />

===Feeding===
Like other finches, the Gouldian finch is a seed eater. They eat up to 30% of their bodyweight each day.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.lady-gouldian-finch-breeding-expert-guide.com/lady-gouldian-finch-diet-feeding.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191222175758/https://www.lady-gouldian-finch-breeding-expert-guide.com/lady-gouldian-finch-diet-feeding.html |archive-date=2019-12-22 |title=Lady Gouldian Finch Diet Feeding Guide|access-date=2019-12-22}}</ref> During the breeding season, Gouldian finches mainly feed on ripe and half-ripe grass seeds of sorghum. During the dry season, they mainly forage on the ground for seeds. During the wet season, spinifex grass seed (''[[Triodia (plant genus)|Triodia]]'' sp.) is an important part of their diet. So far Gouldians have been recorded eating six different species of grass seed, but researchers have yet to find evidence of insect consumption.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/gouldianfinch.html#diet|title=Gouldian Finches or Rainbow Finches {{!}} Beauty of Birds|website=www.beautyofbirds.com|language=en|access-date=2018-08-28}}</ref>

===Breeding===
[[File:Diamant de Gould oisillons.jpg|thumb|right|Newly hatched Gouldian finch chicks (note the light-blue opalescent nodules at the edges of the gape)]]
[[File:YoungGouldianFinch.jpg|thumb|right|A young Gouldian finch (first day out of the nest), still with the blue nodules on the side of its beak]]
Gouldian finches will usually make their nests in tree hollows. They usually breed in the early part of the dry season, when there is plenty of food available.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web | url=https://www.thesprucepets.com/low-maintenance-pet-bird-species-390524 | title=8 Top Low-Maintenance Pet Bird Species}}</ref> When a male is courting a female, he bobs about and ruffles his feathers in an attempt to show off his bright colours. He will expand his chest and fluff out the feathers on his forehead. After mating, the female will lay a clutch of about 4–8 eggs. Both parents help brood the eggs during the daytime, and it is the female who stays on the eggs at night. When the eggs hatch, both parents care for the young. Gouldian finches leave the nest after between 19 and 25 days and are completely independent at 40 days old.<ref name=":1" />

Gouldian finches have brightly coloured gapes and call loudly when the parent birds return so that they are able to find and feed their mouths in the dark nest.<ref>Attenbourgh, David. ''The Life of Birds'', Episode 9, 3 mins, 55 s.</ref>

It has been shown that female Gouldian finches from Northern Australia can control the sex of their offspring by choosing mates according to their head colour. A certain amount of [[genetic incompatibility]] between black and red-headed birds can result in high mortality (up to 80%) in female offspring when birds of different head colours mate. If the female mates with a finch of different head colour, this genetic incompatibility can be addressed by over-producing sons, up to a ratio of four males to one female. This is one of the first proven instances of birds biasing the sex of their offspring to overcome genetic weaknesses.<ref name=pryke>{{cite journal|last1=Pryke|first1=S.R.|last2=Griffith|first2=S.C.|year=2009|title=Genetic incompatibility drives sex allocation and maternal investment in a polymorphic finch|journal=Science|volume=323|issue=5921|pages=1605–1607|doi=10.1126/science.1168928|pmid=19299618|bibcode=2009Sci...323.1605P|s2cid=9348692}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.scientificamerican.com/podcast/episode/bird-controls-offsprings-gender-09-03-20|publisher=Scientific American|title=Bird Controls Offspring's Gender}}</ref>

==Aviculture==
Gouldian finches are a popular species in aviculture because of their striking colours and low care requirements.<ref name=":1" /> Gouldian finches get along well with other species of grass finch and some other docile species of bird, such as [[waxbill]]s and [[parrot finch]]es.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://lafeber.com/pet-birds/species/lady-gouldian-finch/ |title=Gouldian Finch Personality, Food & Care – Pet Birds by Lafeber Co |access-date=28 August 2018 |archive-date=28 August 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180828134724/https://lafeber.com/pet-birds/species/lady-gouldian-finch/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Trapping for aviculture ===
In the Kimberley District of Western Australia, where most wild Gouldian finch were trapped for aviculture, it was often reported as one of the more common of the eleven finch species.  Until 1977, it was trapped in greater numbers than any other finch.  From 1897, when finch trapping started in the Kimberley, it was the most sought after finch by trappers and the most desired by fanciers.  Between 1934 and 1939, the Gouldian finch was the most exported single finch species.  The Perth Zoo exported 22,064 finches of which 12,509 were Gouldian.  Private dealers exported 35,315 finches, of which 14,504 were Gouldian.  The number of finches taken in the 1958 finch trapping season was the largest for one year, of the 38,649 finches taken, 11,286 were Gouldian.  The last licensed trapping of Gouldian finch in Western Australia was on 15 November 1981.  In that year's finch trapping season, of the 23,450 finches taken 1,054 were Gouldian.<ref>Finch Trapping in the Kimberley, K H Coate and L H Merritt (2015) {{ISBN|978-0-85905-621-2}} – ref Fisheries Department, table, trapped birds export statistics 1935–1939 22 August 1939, SROWA CONS 477, ITEM 1934-0059; and Department of Conservation and Land Management, table, Annual Finch Trapping Since 1968 1986.  SROWA CONS 6437, ITEM 014832F3528</ref> However, it is now illegal to export these birds from Australia.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://environment.gov.au/biodiversity/wildlife-trade/natives/exporting-live-australian-natives|title=Department of the Environment and Energy|website=Department of the Environment and Energy|access-date=2018-08-31}}</ref>

== In popular culture ==
The Gouldian finch is used as the basis of the [[ViewSonic]] logo.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.viewsonic.com/us/about-us/|title=Our Vision|website=ViewSonic|access-date=2023-07-21}}</ref>

zh.extract:
胡錦鳥（学名：Chloebia gouldiae），俗稱七彩文鳥，由於種名是英國鳥類學家約翰·古爾德為了紀念已故的妻子伊莉莎白（Elizabeth Gould）而命名，因此又被稱為古爾德雀或格爾丹雀（Gouldian finch）。胡錦鳥是澳洲的特有種，僅零星分布於澳洲大陸北部，分布範圍僅約78.8萬平方公里。由於羽色鮮艷，是很受歡迎的寵物鳥。但據國際鳥盟估計，現今野生胡錦鳥僅剩2,400隻左右，雖然數量尚沒有減少的趨勢，但已被國際自然保護聯盟列為近危物種。
著名的電腦顯示器廠商ViewSonic即是以三隻胡錦鳥作為商標。

斑文鳥
白腰文鳥

黑頭文鳥
十姊妹
文鳥
斑胸草雀


參考文獻
